references.title: Investigating students' use of self‐assessments in higher education using learning analytics

extracted.Courses: 1 Course in a Bachelor's program in Economic and  Business Education at an European University
extracted.Data sources: LMS logs, Self Assessment Results, Questionnaires
extracted.Features: [Ratio of last self-assessment  attempt against best self-assessment possible, ratio of processeed tasks of each self-assessment, attempts per self-assessment, procesing time per self-assessment]
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
Data was cleaned from the LMS during self-assessments (11 in total) - 44 features in total.

All were standardized.
extracted.Performance Metric: Best Model: 2 Clusters were selected based on WCSS and Sillhouette Score
extracted.Cluster Description:
High engagement (n=51): higher engasgement with self-assessments throughout the semester

low-engagement (n=107): lower engagement throughout the semesters.

Both groups have similar engagement for the mid-term test.
extracted.Implications:
Students seem to use self-assessment at specific moments in a semester AND studdents who are more engaged seem to obtain better performance than their peers. 

Learning analytics dashboards should not only draw relationship between engagement and performance and instead should provide process-oriented feedback.
extracted.Comments:
Authors use k-means on a set of students and then check whether there is agreement between the observed data for engagement against:

1. Overall engagement with resources - there is.

2. Alignment with Self-Reported data - there is as well.
extracted.Limitations:
Single course.

Limited number of self-reported variables weere cconsidered. 

Self-Assessments were limited to specific lessons and not framed in a hollistic manner.

No analysis of learner progression.

annotations.text: Formative assessments are a tool that exists but that is often underexplored. The study investigates learner engagement with self-assessments to see if and how they affect student performance. Approach is tested on a sample of students taking a BSc in Economic and Business Education and, throughout the semester were asked to do multiple self-assessments.

Authors used unsupervised and process mining approaches on the self-assessment records and found that most formative assessments were explored in preparation for the summative assessments. In general, 2 main clusters emerged and the obtained results were consistent with the self-reported strategies adopted.
